name: Mock Test - 05
instruction: This question paper contains three sections – A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. 3 . Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: Given an arbitrary equivalence relation R in an arbitrary set X, R divides X into
questions.question_html: Given an arbitrary equivalence relation R in an arbitrary set X, R divides X into
questions.explanation: An equivalence relation R gives a partitioning of the set A into mutually disjoint equivalence classes,i.e. union of equivalence classes is the set A itself.

questions.question_text: Let R be the feasible region (convex polygon) for a linear programming problem and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. When Z has an optimal value (maximum or minimum), where the variables x and y are subject to constraints described by linear inequalities,
questions.question_html: Let R be the feasible region (convex polygon) for a linear programming problem and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. When Z has an optimal value (maximum or minimum), where the variables x and y are subject to constraints described by linear inequalities,

options.text: optimal value must occur at the midpoints of the corner points (vertices) of the feasible region.
options.html: optimal value must occur at the midpoints of the corner points (vertices) of the feasible region.

options.text: optimal value must occur at the endpoints of the corner points (vertices) of the feasible region.
options.html: optimal value must occur at the endpoints of the corner points (vertices) of the feasible region.

options.text: optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.
options.html: optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.

options.text: optimal value must occur at the centroid of the feasible region.
options.html: optimal value must occur at the centroid of the feasible region.

questions.correct_option_text: optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.
questions.explanation: Let R be the feasible region (convex polygon) for a linear programming problem and let Z = ax + by be the objective function. When Z has an optimal value (maximum or minimum), where the variables x and y are subject to constraints described by linear inequalities then , optimal value must occur at a corner point (vertex) of the feasible region.

questions.question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 3 x}{x}, & x \neq 0 \\ \frac{k}{2}, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then k =
questions.question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 3 x}{x}, & x \neq 0 \\ \frac{k}{2}, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then k =
questions.explanation: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 3 x}{x}=\frac{k}{2}{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 3 x}{3 x} \times 3=\frac{k}{2}{/tex} {tex}3=\frac{k}{2}{/tex} k = 6

questions.question_text: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then det (adj(adj A)) is
questions.question_html: If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then det (adj(adj A)) is
questions.explanation: {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = 14 det( adjA)= det(A) 3-1 = det(A) 2 .Here the operation is done two times.so, det (adj(adj A)) = {tex}|A|^{(n-1)^{2}}{/tex} det (adj(adj A)) = {tex}14^{(3-1)^{2}}=14^{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Minimize Z = 5x + 10 y subject to x + 2y ≤ 120, x + y ≥ 60, x – 2y ≥ 0, x, y ≥ 0
questions.question_html: Minimize Z = 5x + 10 y subject to x + 2y ≤ 120, x + y ≥ 60, x – 2y ≥ 0, x, y ≥ 0
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = 5x + 10 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : x + 2y ≤ 120, x + y ≥ 60, x – 2y ≥ 0, x, y ≥ 0 . The corner points are obtained by drawing the lines x+2y =120, x+y = 60 and x-2y = 0. The points so obtained are (60,30),(120,0), (60,0) and (40,20) Corner points Z = 5x + 10y D(60 ,30 ) 600 A(120,0) 600 B(60,0) 300……………………..(Min.) C(40,20) 400 Here , Z = 300 is minimum at ( 60, 0 ).

questions.question_text: The radius of a sphere is changing at the rate of 0.1 cm/sec. The rate of change of its surface area when the radius is 200 cm is
questions.question_html: The radius of a sphere is changing at the rate of 0.1 cm/sec. The rate of change of its surface area when the radius is 200 cm is
questions.explanation: {tex}S=4 \pi r^{2}{/tex} differentiating with respect to t {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}=8 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}{/tex} {tex}r=200 \mathrm{cm}, \frac{d r}{d t}=0.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d t}=8 \pi \times 200 \times 0.1=160 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} b+c & a & a \\ b & c+a & b \\ c & c & a+b \end{array}\right|{/tex} = ?
questions.question_html: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} b+c & a & a \\ b & c+a & b \\ c & c & a+b \end{array}\right|{/tex} = ?
questions.explanation: Apply R 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R 1 - (R 2 + R 3 ) Take (-2 ) common from R 1 . Apply R 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (R 2 - R 1 ) and R 3 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (R 3 - R 1 )

questions.question_text: If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix and B is a matrix such that A'B and AB' are both defined, then the order of the matrix B is:
questions.question_html: If A is a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 matrix and B is a matrix such that A'B and AB' are both defined, then the order of the matrix B is:
questions.explanation: We have to find: Order of A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 Order of A’ = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 As A T B and BA T are both defined, so the number of columns in B should be equal to the number of rows in A’ for BA’ and also the number of columns in A’ should be equal to the number of rows in A’ for BA’. Therefore, the order of matrix B is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.

questions.question_text: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\log \left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\log \left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right){/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\log \left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{\left( {\left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right)}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{{\left( {\left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right)}}\frac{1}{2}{\sec ^2}\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1}{{2\sin \frac{x}{2}\cos \frac{x}{2}}} = \frac{1}{{\sin x}} = cosec x {/tex}

questions.question_text: Corner points of the feasible region for an LPP are (0, 2), (3, 0), (6, 0), (6, 8) and (0, 5).Let F = 4x + 6y be the objective function. The Minimum value of F occurs at
questions.question_html: Corner points of the feasible region for an LPP are (0, 2), (3, 0), (6, 0), (6, 8) and (0, 5).Let F = 4x + 6y be the objective function. The Minimum value of F occurs at

options.text: any point on the line segment joining the points (0, 2) and (3, 0).
options.html: any point on the line segment joining the points (0, 2) and (3, 0).

options.text: the mid-point of the line segment joining the points (0, 2) and (3, 0) only
options.html: the mid-point of the line segment joining the points (0, 2) and (3, 0) only

questions.correct_option_text: any point on the line segment joining the points (0, 2) and (3, 0).
questions.explanation: Here the objective function is given by : F = 4x +6y. Corner points Corresponding value of F = 4x + 6y (0, 2) 12 {tex}\leftarrow{/tex} Minimum (3, 0) 12 {tex}\leftarrow{/tex} Minimum (6, 0) 24 (6, 8) 72 (0, 5) 30 Hence it is clear that the minimum value occurs at any point on the line joining the points (0,2) and (3,0)

questions.question_text: The function f(x) = {tex}\frac{- x }2 {/tex} + sin x defined on [ {tex}\frac{- n }3 , \frac{71} 3{/tex} ] is

questions.explanation: increasing

questions.question_text: Let f(x) = (x + |x|) |x|. Then, for all x
questions.question_html: Let f(x) = (x + |x|) |x|. Then, for all x

options.text: f is differentiable for some x
options.html: f is differentiable for some x

questions.explanation: Given that f(x) = (x + |x|) |x| {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{c} (\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x})(-\mathrm{x}), \mathrm{x}<0 \\ 0, \mathrm{x}=0 \\ (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x})(\mathrm{x}), \mathrm{x}>0 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{c} 0, \mathrm{x}<0 \\ 0, \mathrm{x}=0 \\ 2 \mathrm{x}^{2}, \mathrm{x}>0 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} So, we can say that f(x) is continuous for all x. Now, checking the differentiability at x =0 Given that f(x) = (x + |x|) |x| {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{c} (\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x})(-\mathrm{x}), \mathrm{x}<0 \\ 0, \mathrm{x}=0 \\ (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x})(\mathrm{x}), \mathrm{x}>0 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{c} 0, \mathrm{x}<0 \\ 0, \mathrm{x}=0 \\ 2 \mathrm{x}^{2}, \mathrm{x}>0 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} So, we can say that f(x) is continuous for all x. Now, checking the differentiability at x =0 LHD at x =0, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{0-h-0}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{0-(0)}{-h}=0{/tex} RHD at x =0, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{0+h-0}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}-(0)}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h}{1}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHD = RHD So, f(x) is differentiable for all x. LHD at x =0, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{0-h-0}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{0-(0)}{-h}=0{/tex} RHD at x =0, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{0+h-0}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h^{2}-(0)}{h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 h}{1}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHD = RHD So, f(x) is differentiable for all x.

questions.question_text: The points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{5}\left(2 x^{2}+3\right), & x \leq 1 \\ 6-5 x & , 1<x<3 \\ x-3 & , x \geq 3 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is (are)
questions.question_html: The points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{5}\left(2 x^{2}+3\right), & x \leq 1 \\ 6-5 x & , 1<x<3 \\ x-3 & , x \geq 3 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is (are)
questions.explanation: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} \frac{1}{5}\left(2 x^{2}+3\right)=1{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} 6-5 x=1{/tex} Function is continuous at x = 1 {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^-} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} 6-5 x=-9{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow{3^+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} x-3=0{/tex} Function is discontinuous at x = 3

questions.question_text: The sides of an equilateral triangle are increasing at the rate of 2 cm/sec. The rate at which the area increases, when side is 10 cm is:
questions.question_html: The sides of an equilateral triangle are increasing at the rate of 2 cm/sec. The rate at which the area increases, when side is 10 cm is:
questions.explanation: Let the side of an equilateral triangle be x cm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of equilateral triangle, {tex}A=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} ...(i) Also, {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 2 cm/s (given) Differentiating equation (i) w.r.t. 't', we get {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot 2 x \cdot \frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{\sqrt{3} x d x}{d t}{/tex} When x = 10 {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{10 \sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot 2=10 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If the curves y = 2e x and y = ae -x intersect orthogonally, then a =
questions.question_html: If the curves y = 2ex and y = ae-x intersect orthogonally, then a =
questions.explanation: y = 2e x and y = ae -x {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1}=2 e^{x}{/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-a e^{-x}{/tex} Given that {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{1} \times\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{2}=-1{/tex} 2e x {tex}\times{/tex} -ae -x = -1 2a = 1 {tex}a=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If |x| {tex}\le{/tex} 1, then 2 tan –1 x + sin –1 {tex}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If |x| {tex}\le{/tex} 1, then 2 tan–1 x + sin–1 {tex}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: We need to find, 2 tan 1 x + sin -1 {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} We know that, 2tan -1 p = sin -1 {tex}\frac{2 p}{1+p^{2}}{/tex} So, 2tan 1 x + sin -1 {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 tan 1 x + 2 tan -1 x = 4 tan -1 x

questions.question_text: If x = f (t) cos t - f '(t) sint and y = f (t) sin t + f '(t) cost, then {tex}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)^{2}{/tex} =
questions.question_html: If x = f (t) cos t - f '(t) sint and y = f (t) sin t + f '(t) cost, then {tex}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)^{2}{/tex} =
questions.explanation: x = f(t) cos t - f(t) sin t, and y = f(t) sint + f(t) cos t Now, x cos t + y sin t = f(t) (cos 2 t + sin 2 t) = f(t) ..(i) x sin t - y cos t = -f'(t) (cos 2 t + sin 2 t) = -f'(t)...(ii) Differentiating (i) and (ii) w.r.t. to t we get {tex}\frac{d x}{d t} \cos t-x \sin t+\frac{d y}{d t} \sin t+y \cos t=f^{\prime}(t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t} \cos t+\frac{d y}{d t} \sin t=0{/tex} .......(iii) and {tex}\frac{d x}{d t} \sin t+x \cos t-\frac{d y}{d t} \cos t+y \sin t=-f^{\prime \prime}(t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t} \sin t-\frac{d y}{d t} \cos t=-f^{\prime \prime}(t)-f(t){/tex} ...(iv) Solving (iii) and (iv) we get {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -sin t (f "(t) + f(t)) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = cos t (f "(t) + f(t)) {tex}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)^{2}=\left(f^{\prime \prime}(t)+f(t)\right)^{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The diameter of a circle is increasing at the rate of 1 cm/sec. When its radius is {tex}\pi{/tex} , the rate of increase of its area is
questions.question_html: The diameter of a circle is increasing at the rate of 1 cm/sec. When its radius is {tex}\pi{/tex}, the rate of increase of its area is
questions.explanation: {tex}A=\pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A=\pi\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d t}=2 \pi \frac{D}{4} \frac{d D}{d t}{/tex} {tex}given \;r=\pi \Rightarrow D=2 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=2 \pi \times \frac{2 \pi}{4} \times 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\pi^{2} \mathrm{cm}^{2} / \mathrm{sec}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A function f: X {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Y is said to be one – one and onto if
questions.question_html: A function f: X {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Y  is said to be one – one and onto if

options.text: f is either one – one or onto
options.html: f is either one – one or onto

options.text: f is both one – one and onto
options.html: f is both one – one and onto

questions.correct_option_text: f is both one – one and onto
questions.explanation: A function f: X {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Y is defined to be one – one (or injective), if f {tex}{x_1} \ne {x_2}{/tex} in X {tex} \Rightarrow f({x_1}) \ne f({x_2}){/tex} in Y. and {tex}{R_f} = {\text{ }}Y.{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let f(x) = (x - a) 2 + (x - b) 2 + (x - c) 2 . Then f(x) has a minimum at x =
questions.question_html: Let f(x) = (x - a)2 + (x - b)2 + (x - c)2. Then f(x) has a minimum at x =
questions.explanation: f(x) = (x - a) 2 + (x - b) 2 + (x - c) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2(x - a) + 2(x - b) + 2(x - c) to find minima or maxima f'(x) = 0 2(x - a) + 2(x - b) + 2(x - c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{a+b+c}{3}{/tex} f"(x) = 6 > 0 function has minima at x = {tex}\frac{a+b+c}{3}{/tex} .

questions.question_text: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. Let M and m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points. In case feasible region is unbounded, M is the maximum value of the objective function if
questions.question_html: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. Let M and m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points. In case feasible region is unbounded, M is the maximum value of the objective function if

options.text: The open half plane determined by ax + by > M has no point in common with the feasible region
options.html: The open half plane determined by ax + by > M has no point in common with the feasible region

options.text: The open half plane determined by ax + by < M has no point in common with the feasible region
options.html: The open half plane determined by ax + by < M has no point in common with the feasible region

options.text: The open half plane determined by ax + by > M has points in common with the feasible region
options.html: The open half plane determined by ax + by > M has points in common with the feasible region

options.text: The open half plane determined by ax + by < M has points in common with the feasible region
options.html: The open half plane determined by ax + by < M has points in common with the feasible region

questions.correct_option_text: The open half plane determined by ax + by > M has no point in common with the feasible region
questions.explanation: In Corner point method for solving a linear programming problem one finds the feasible region of the linear programming problem ,determines its corner points and evaluates the objective function Z = ax + by at each corner point. Let M and m respectively be the largest and smallest values at corner points. In case feasible region is unbounded, M is the maximum value of the objective function if the open half plane determined by ax + by > M has no point in common with the feasible region . Otherwise, Z has no maximum value.

questions.question_text: The function f (x) = x 2 , for all real x, is
questions.question_html: The function f (x) = x2, for all real x, is
questions.explanation: f(x) = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f'(x) = 2x for all x in R. Since f ‘(x) = 2x > 0 for x >0, and f ‘ (x) = 2x< 0 for x < 0, therefore on R, f is neither increasing nor decreasing. Infact , f is strict increasing on [ 0, {tex}\infty {/tex} ) and strict decreasing on (- {tex}\infty ,0\;{/tex} ].

questions.question_text: The differential coefficient of log (|log x|) w.r.t. log x is
questions.question_html: The differential coefficient of log (|log x|) w.r.t. log x is
questions.explanation: Let y = log(|logx|) and z = logx, then we have; {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dz}} = \frac{{\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}{{\frac{{dz}}{{dx}}}} = \left( {\frac{1}{{\log x}}.\frac{1}{x}} \right)/\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{\log x}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The value of {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right) - {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{a - b}}{{a + b}}} \right){/tex} is {tex}\left( {a,\,\,b > 0} \right){/tex}
questions.question_html: The value of {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right) - {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{a - b}}{{a + b}}} \right){/tex}is {tex}\left( {a,\,\,b > 0} \right){/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{a}{b}} \right) - {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{a - b}}{{a + b}}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\frac{a}{b} - \frac{{a - b}}{{a + b}}}}{{1 + \frac{a}{b}.\frac{{a - b}}{{a + b}}}}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{a^2} + ab - ab + {b^2}}}{{ab + {b^2} + {a^2} - ab}}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}(1) = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let S be the set of all straight lines in a plane. Let R be a relation on S defined by {tex}L R M \Leftrightarrow L \perp M{/tex} Then R is
questions.question_html: Let S be the set of all straight lines in a plane. Let R be a relation on S defined by {tex}L R M \Leftrightarrow L \perp M{/tex} Then R is

options.text: transitive but neither reflexive nor symmetric
symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
options.html: symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive

options.text: reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
options.html: reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive

questions.correct_option_text: symmetric but neither reflexive nor transitive
questions.explanation: Since the line L is not perpendicular to itself so the relation is not reflexive. If the line L is perpendicular to line M then the line M is also perpendicular to line L to the relation is symmetric. If line L is perpendicular to line M and M is perpendicular to line F then Line L and Line F are parallel to each other so the relation is not transitive.

questions.question_text: The principal value of sin -1 {tex}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The principal value of sin-1 {tex}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let x = sin -1 {tex}\left(\sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = sin {tex}\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right){/tex} Here the range of principle value of sine is {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3} \notin\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} Hence, for all values of x in range {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} , the value of sin -1 {tex}\left(\sin (\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)){/tex} is {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = sin {tex}\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} sin {tex}(\frac{2 \pi}{3}){/tex} = sin {tex}\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin x = sin {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} sin ( {tex}\pi-\theta{/tex} ) = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} as here {tex}\theta{/tex} lies in II quadrant and sine is positive) {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&9 \\ 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 4&{16}&{64} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} , then
questions.question_html: If {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&9 \\ 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 4&{16}&{64} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex}{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex}, then
questions.explanation: {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&9 \\ 1&x&{{x^2}} \\ 4&{16}&{64} \end{array}} \right| = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} because, the value of the determinant is zero only when the two of its rows or column are identical, which is possible only when either x = 3 or x = 4 .

questions.question_text: If f is derivable at x = a , then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to a} \;\;\frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If f is derivable at x = a , then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to a} \;\;\frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to a} \frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}} \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{(a + h)f(a) - af(a + h)}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left\{ {\frac{{hf(a)}}{h} - \frac{{af(a + h) -a f(a)}}{h}} \right\} = f(a) - af'(a) {/tex}

questions.question_text: If x sin (a + y) = sin y, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If x sin (a + y) = sin y, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} x\sin (a + y) = \sin y\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{\sin y}}{{\sin (a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin (a + y)\cos y - \sin y\cos (a + y)}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sin (a + y - y)}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}} = \frac{{\operatorname{sina} }}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The slope of the tangent to the curve x = t 2 + 3t - 8, y = 2t 2 - 2t - 5 at the point (2,-1) is
questions.question_html: The slope of the tangent to the curve x = t2 + 3t - 8, y = 2t2 - 2t - 5 at the point (2,-1) is
questions.explanation: It is given that curve x = t 2 + 3t - 8, y = 2t 2 - 2t - 5 Then, {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}=2 t+3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=4 t-2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{4 t-2}{2 t+3}{/tex} The given point is (2, -1) So, At x = 2, we get t 2 + 3t - 8 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t 2 + 3t - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (t - 2)(t + 5) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 2 and -5 At y = -1, we get 2t 2 - 2t - 5 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2t 2 - 2t - 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(t 2 - t - 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (t - 2)(t + 1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 2 and -1 Therefore, the common value of t is 2. Hence, the slope of the tangent to the given curve at point (2, - 1) is {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right|_{t=2}=\frac{4(2)-2}{2(2)+3}=\frac{8-2}{4+3}=\frac{6}{7}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The system of equations x + y + z = 2, 3 x - y + 2z = 6 3x + y + z = -18 has:
questions.question_html:
The system of equations
x + y + z = 2,
3 x - y + 2z = 6  
3x + y + z = -18
has:

options.text: zero solution as the only solution
options.html: zero solution as the only solution

questions.explanation: a unique solution The given system of equations can be written in matrix form as follows: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 6 \\ -18 \end{array}\right]{/tex} AX = B Here, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 6 \\ -18 \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = 1(- 1 - 2) - 1 (-3 - 6) + 1(3 + 3) = -3 + 3 + 6 = 6 not equal to 0. So, the given system of equations has a unique solution.

questions.question_text: If the radius of a sphere is measures as 7m with an error of 0.02m then find the approximate error in calculating its volume.
questions.question_html: If the radius of a sphere is measures as 7m with an error of 0.02m then find the approximate error in calculating its volume.
questions.explanation: Given , radius of the sphere is 7 m Error in the measurement of radius = {tex}\Delta r{/tex} = 0.02 m We have volume of sphere = {tex}V={ \frac { 4 }{ 3 } }\pi { r }^{ 3 }{/tex} Now, {tex}dV=\left( \frac { dV }{ dx } \right) \triangle r={ \frac { 4 }{ 3 } }\pi .3{ r }^{ 2 }\triangle r=4\pi { r }^{ 2 }.\triangle r{/tex} {tex}\ =4\pi \times 49\times 0.02=3.92\pi \;{ m }^{ 3 }{/tex}

questions.question_text: The derivative of the function {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{(\cos 2 x)^{1 / 2}\right\}{/tex} at x = {tex}\pi{/tex} /6 is
questions.question_html: The derivative of the function {tex}\cot ^{-1}\left\{(\cos 2 x)^{1 / 2}\right\}{/tex} at x = {tex}\pi{/tex}/6 is
questions.explanation: {tex}f(x)=\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos 2 x}){/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-1}{1+\cos 2 x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\cos 2 x}} \times-2 \sin 2 x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{-1}{2 \cos ^{2} x} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\cos 2 x}} \times-2 \sin 2 x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{1}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{6}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\cos \frac{\pi}{3}}} \times 2 \sin \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{3}{4}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}} \times 2 \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}

Equivalence classes Ai satisfy

  3. Some elements of Ai are related to any element of Aj, {tex},{\text{ }}i{\text{ }} \ne {\text{ }}j{/tex}
  4. All elements of Ai are related to any element of Aj,{tex}{\text{ }}i{\text{ }} \ne {\text{ }}j{/tex}i are related to any element of Aj,{tex}{\text{ }}i{\text{ }} \ne {\text{ }}j{/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}{A_1},{\text{ }}{A_{2{\text{ }},}}{A_{3{\text{ }}, \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots .}}{A_{k}}{/tex} be subsets of a set A such that {tex}\bigcup\limits_{i = 1}^n {{A_i} = A} {/tex} and {tex}{A_i} \cap {A_j} = \phi {/tex} for {tex}i{\text{ }} \ne {\text{ }}j{\text{ }}{/tex}

questions.question_html: If {tex}3{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \right) - 4{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - {x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \right) + 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x}}{{1 - {x^2}}}} \right) = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex}. Then, x =
questions.explanation: We have to find: {tex}3{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \right) - 4{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - {x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \right) + 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2x}}{{1 - {x^2}}}} \right) = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}3{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right) - 4{\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right) + 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right)\,\, = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} {tex}3{\sin ^{ - 1}}(\sin 2\theta )\, - 4{\cos ^{ - 1}}(\cos 2\theta ) + 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\tan 2\theta } \right)\, = \frac{\pi }{3}{/tex} {tex}3.2\theta \, - 4.2\theta \, + 2.2\theta \, = \,\frac{\pi }{3}\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow 2\theta = \frac{\pi }{3}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \,\,{\tan ^{ - 1}}x = \frac{\pi }{6}\,\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,x = \tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{6}} \right)\,\, = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Maximize Z = 3x + 2y subject to x + 2y ≤ 10, 3x + y ≤ 15, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Maximize Z = 3x + 2y subject to x + 2y ≤ 10, 3x + y ≤ 15, x, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = 3x + 2 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : x + 2y ≤ 10, 3x + y ≤ 15, x, y ≥ 0. The corner points obtained by drawing the lines 3x+y=15 and x+2y=10 graphically are (0,0),(0,5), (5,0) and (4,3). Corner points Z = 3x + 2y O(0 ,0 ) 0 A(5,0) 15 B(0,5) 10 C(4,3) 18……………………..(Max.) Here , Z = 18 is maximum at ( 4, 3 ).

questions.question_text: Let {tex}\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{ax}^{2}+1 & , \mathrm{x}>1 \\ \mathrm{x}+1 / 2 & , \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Then, f(x) is derivable at x = 1, is
questions.question_html: Let {tex}\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{ax}^{2}+1 & , \mathrm{x}>1 \\ \mathrm{x}+1 / 2 & , \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}Then, f(x) is derivable at x = 1, is
questions.explanation: Given function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} a x^{2}+1, x>1 \\ x+\frac{1}{2}, x \leq 1 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} and f(x) is derivable at x = 1. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHD (at x = 1) = RHD (at x = 1) {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{1-h-1}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{1+h-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(1-h+\frac{1}{2}\right)-\frac{3}{2}}{-h}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{a(1+h)^{2}+1-\frac{3}{2}}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}-\frac{1}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. Then number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) is
questions.question_html: Let A = {1, 2, 3}. Then number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) is
questions.explanation: It is given that A = {1, 2, 3}. An equivalence relation is reflexive, symmetric and transitive. The smallest equivalence relations containing (1, 2) is equal to R 1 = {(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 1)} Now, only four pairs are left (2, 3), (3, 2), (1, 3) and (3, 1). So, if we add the pair (2, 3) to R, then for symmetry we must add (3, 2). Also, for transitivity we required to add (1, 3) and (3, 1). Thus, the only equivalence relation is the universal relation. Therefore, the total number of equivalence relations containing (1, 2) is 2.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children. The requirement of calories and proteins for each person in matrix form can be represented as
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children.
 


The requirement of calories and proteins for each person in matrix form can be represented as
questions.explanation: Let F be the matrix representing the number of family members and R be the matrix representing the requirement of calories and proteins for each person. Then F = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{ccc}& Men \ Women \ Children\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}4&4&4\\2&2&2\end{bmatrix}}{/tex} R = {tex}\begin{array}{c}Man\\woman\\Children\end{array}\overset{\begin{array}{cc}Calories&Proteins\end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}2400&45\\1900&55\\1800&33\end{bmatrix}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children. The requirement of calories of family A is
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children.
 


The requirement of calories of family A is
questions.explanation: The requirement of calories and proteins for each of the two families is given by the product matrix FR. FR = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2400 & 45 \\ 1900 & 55 \\ 1800 & 33 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 4(2400+1900+1800) & 4(45+55+33) \\ 2(2400+1900+1800) & 2(45+55+33) \end{array}\right]{/tex} FR = {tex}\overset{\begin{array}{c}Calories\;Proteins \end{array}}{\begin{bmatrix}24400&532\\12200&266\end{bmatrix}\begin{array}{c}Family\;A\\Family\;B\end{array}}{/tex}

The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children. If A and B are two matrices such that AB = B and BA = A, then A 2 + B 2 equals
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children.
 


If A and B are two matrices such that AB = B and BA = A, then A2 + B2 equals
questions.explanation: Since, AB = B ...(i) and BA = A ..(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 + B 2 = A {tex}\cdot{/tex} A + B {tex}\cdot{/tex} B = A(BA) + B(AB) [using (i) and (ii)] - (AB)A + (BA)B [Associative law] = BA + AB [using (i) and (ii)] = A + B

questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 46 to 50:

Consider 2 families A and B. Suppose there are 4 men, 4 women and 4 children in family A and 2 men, 2 women and 2 children in family B. The recommended daily amount of calories is 2400 for a man, 1900 for a woman, 1800 for children and 45 grams of proteins for a man, 55 grams for a woman and 33 grams for children.
 


If A = (aij)m {tex}\times{/tex} n, B = (bij)n {tex}\times{/tex} p and C = (cij)p {tex}\times{/tex} q, then the product (BC)A is possible only when
questions.explanation: A = (a ij ) m {tex}\times{/tex} n , B = (b ij ) n {tex}\times{/tex} p , C = (c ij ) p {tex}\times{/tex} q BC = (b ij ) n {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} (C ij ) p {tex}\times{/tex} q = (d ij ) n {tex}\times{/tex} q (BC)A = (d ij ) n {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} (a ij ) m {tex}\times{/tex} M Hence, (BC)A is possible only when m = q
